title: The Macro Minute: Is it too late to position for Paradigm C?

Howdy team 42. It's your skipper here at
Darius Dell to present our macro minute
for Tuesday, May 13, 2025. Hope everyone
had a wonderful Mother's Day weekend
with their moms and their uh their
wives, their sisters and their all their
aunts, etc. Uh just spreading the love.
So um getting into uh our executive
summary from today's leadoff morning
note. As always, if you want to review
the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's schem macro question is is it
too late to position for paradigm C? Our
answers are stocks and bullish sentiment
continued higher Tuesday after a 3.3%
meltup in the S&P 500 on Monday called
bears offsides in response to the 90-day
US China tariff truce. US Treasury
Secretary Scott Bessant called the
90-day tariff pause a very good
framework for future talks at the Saudi
US investment forum in Riad. Vesson also
uh indicated to investors that the
deescalation was stronger than even the
best hopes. The administration's strong
desire to celebrate wins on the trade
negotiation front is consistent with our
long-held belief that the bond market
broke President Trump on April 9th and
forced his administration to pivot away
from tolerate tolerating the pain
required to transition to paradigm B to
celebrating the gains of an
investor-friendly transition to paradigm
C. All told, incremental validation of
our paradigm C thesis suggests there is
likely upside for both stocks and
earnings from here. Per the latest Bank
of America fund manager survey,
institutional investors were a net 38%
underweight US stocks heading into last
weekend's US China trade war dant.
Another sign of the bullish paradigm C
times is Coinbase rallying 16% today on
news that it will replace Discover in
the S&P 500. These types of gappy up
moves in names that are further out on
the risk spectrum. Signal investors
still have plenty of dry powder to
invest. Our channel checks indicate
still elevated policy uncertainty is
keeping investors that mistakenly rely
solely on fundamental signals to manage
risk more underinvested than they likely
should be at the current juncture. As
such, we remarked a few weeks back on
our friend Charles Payne's show, Making
Money. By the time that uncertainty is
fully resolved, it will be too late to
position for and participate in upside
risk. Transition to our 42 micro
dashboard is always a wrap- up with a
question from our community. This one's
titled, "What can go wrong in paradigm
C?" Says, "It appears we're getting um
confirmation after confirmation that
Trump is adopting what Bessant laid out
in his January 2024 letter to investors
on a Trump 2.0 0 term would look like
with a focus less and less on tariffs
and used for negotiations and more on a
focus of and more of if we focus on a
weak dollar to create a domestic
economic boom. So I imagine the goal
from here is to grow our way into a more
responsible deficit range by the end of
this term. I have to think this is
bullish for risk assets. But what could
go wrong from here? Is it that the risk
that the Fed ends up uh having to raise
rates as opposed to lower with inflation
inevitably rising? I could see Jay Pal
doing this, but whoever Trump appoints
next year has to be someone willing to
kiss the ring. Can the bond market wreck
these plans? Ultimately, what could go
wrong um here in Paradigm C? Side note,
I'm curious if Trump claiming he will
balance the budget in his first joint
address to Congress was written in his
speech or if he said that off the cuff
because I don't see a world uh where
that is possible. So, um you know,
thoughtful question, a lot to unpack
here. So let me just kind of go through
it u um you know kind of in order here
just to make sure the uh the answers are
are salient. Um so on this um on this um
kind of with regards to Scott Besson's
January 2024 letter and with you know
this Trump 2.0 term is going to have
less focus on tariffs and more of a
focus on weak dollar. I don't know if I
agree with that. I mean we our effective
tariff rate has been taken back to a a
level that is consistent with smooth
holly. So, um I think that that's
probably I wouldn't agree with that
characterization. Um this sort of focus
on a weak dollar in my opinion is I
think hard to do. uh because on one hand
we've had a very strong US dollar and
that's been persistent for almost 15
years now as a function of the capital
inflows that we've we've hoover vacuumed
into the US economy partially as a
function of US exceptionalism but more
as a function of our econ our fiscal
monetary large s that created the US
exceptionalism that needed to be
capitalized by the rest of the world
which ultimately led to our twin deficit
position of 11% of GDP you know when
you're talking about these sorts of
macroeconomic vulnerabilities like twin
deficits, you know, structural budget
deficit of 7%, you know, um current
account deficit of 4%. These are
vulnerabilities that, you know, can
persist for an indefinite period of time
unless you have a trigger to start to
unwind those things. And it appeared at
the beginning of this transition process
when the the Trump administration was,
you know, very keen or at least signal
that they were keen to bully the world
into paradigm B that that was a trigger
to start to unwind those types of
vulnerabilities and imbalances. Uh
however, ever since April 9th, it's been
our view that they are pivoting us
towards paradigm C uh which is
essentially a continuation of the same
thing from a fiscal and monetary large S
perspective, but also some incremental
upside, you know, supply side sweeteners
that will boost growth even further. And
so in my opinion, I don't really see how
you can have a sustained dollarbear
market in paradigm C to the extent that
paradigm C persists um you know,
maintains the US economic
exceptionalism. And in our view it will
be maintained if we can maintain the
fiscal and monetary large s um that is
supported that that is that that
exceptionalism is born out of again you
know 7% budget deficit to GDP year after
year after year and you're producing
you're consuming 4% of your GDP is worth
more than you produce every year or
you're saving 4% of your GDP is less
than you're investing every year. Uh
those are two sides of the current
account um deficit coin. If you're doing
all that, of course, you're going to be
exceptional economically. And so, in my
opinion, it's going to be difficult to
have a durable dollarbear market uh in
paradigm C. Doesn't mean the dollar
can't go down, but I think it's a lot
more two-sided risk from here uh in that
view. Um and so, getting back into the
question, I have to think this is
bullish from risk assets. What can go
wrong from here? Uh the number one thing
we think could go wrong from here is if
the Trump
administration sees markets pricing in
paradigm C and and mistakenly takes that
as a signal that they should you know
that that it's okay to go back to
bullying the world into paradigm B
again. Um I don't think that's a high
probability outcome. Um just given that
the administration has broadly sort of
pivoted to this kind of progrowth pro
markets um phase of their agenda. uh in
our opinion we think the detox period
that you know Besson was warning about
back in February and uh the period of
disturbance economic restructuring that
you know various men members of the
administration were signaling back in
you know February in our opinion you
know it just seems like that's over um
at least according to our our our views
and and all the signals that we've
received from the administration since
then so I don't know that pivoting back
towards paradigm B is a high probability
outcome but to me that's the number one
risk right if they you know just look at
the market performance and go well
markets are doing well again so if we
want to get more and more headway. If we
want to accomplish more on the trade
policy front, let's go back to bullying
the world in a paradigm V. So that that
to me it's the number one risk. Uh it's
impossible to quantify that risk or
appropriately handicapped that risk. But
again, not every risk in financial
markets can be appropriately quantified
and handicapped. At some point, you just
have to take risk as an investor to make
money in financial markets. You can't
you there's never going to have a
guaranteed amount of certainty. And as
we've been talking about, I said on
Charles Payne show, I said it again this
morning. by the time there's no
uncertainty, the markets will have
already moved. You know, you can't sit
around and wait for certainty as an
investor. Um, so this is um sometimes
you just have to make, you know, you
know, just go with the highest
probability decision whether or not
sometimes that high probability decision
is, you know, 75 80 90% in terms of your
assessment of that probability, but
other times the highest probability
decision is 51%. So what do you do with
that? Well, you just got to do what you
got to do as an investor and and take
risk. Uh and so uh this is why we get
paid by financial markets for taking
risk because uh this is hard. Um,
getting back into the question, is there
a risk that uh the Fed ends up having to
raise rates as opposed to lower that
with inflation inevitably rising? Uh,
less so. I think the Fed having to raise
rates is more of a capital outflow story
that's born out of a sustained dollar
bear market or sorry, a sustained dollar
bear would be really a function of the
capital outflow story and the Fed would
likely have to uh keep rates elevated
and or raise rates to uh prevent that
kind of um, you know, that that those
kinds of capital outflows or curtail
them to some degree. Uh again, we saw
this on several occasions throughout the
month of April, uh when we saw, you
know, various instances of this Lame
American trade. Now, again, I don't
think that's where we're headed anymore.
I'm just letting you know that u you I
generally agree with this kind of
sentiment. Uh and then, uh getting back
into the question, uh can the bond
market wreck those plans? Uh ultimately,
what could go wrong for me in Paradise
C? I think that's a very
underappreciated risk in the bond market
is that um you know Trump the Trump
administration uh taps you know
essentially what will be a psy to to to
replace uh JP pal as the federal
reserve. J pal in my opinion you know
throughout several times in this uh in
this in this process over the past kind
of six weeks or so. He was the only
thing standing in between the US bond
market term premier from rising another
100 basis points a and and the bond
market prices being fairly contained.
Obviously, they were not on on April
9th, on April 11th or April 21st, but um
generally speaking, we've had some
fairly tame uh uh uh uh uh bond pricing
uh in the long end of the Treasury bond
market. uh and though that that pricing
will get very much uncontained in my
opinion uh if uh the Trump
administration appoints someone that is
going to essentially run a you know kind
of a structurally weak dollar policy in
the sense that they have policy rates
much much much lower uh than they
otherwise would be uh according to the
Taylor rule or just broader you know
economic and financial conditions. Uh so
just be aware of that. That is
definitely a risk to the long end of the
curve from an inflation expectations uh
standpoint. And also you got the term
premier um issue that in my opinion is
very unlikely to go away. We're seeing
some fiscal retrenchment as a function
of Doge. But you know even if Doge is
successful at accomplishing its entire
um trillion dollar uh target, which in
our opinion we don't think they're going
to be or or even want to be anymore, but
even if they are successful at at at
cutting a trillion dollars out of
annualized federal expenditures, you're
talking about a budget deficit that's
going to wind up at 5% from 7% prior and
then it'll start to grow again as a
function of two things. one the tax cut
program that the the congressional
Republicans are legislating talking
about $4 trillion in tax cuts but only
1.5 or even less trillion dollars in
spending cuts. So you're going to start
to add to budget deficits again from
that starting point. Uh at the same time
you also have some of these politically
protected categories that aren't going
to get cut like social security,
Medicaid, national defense, debt
interest, uh uh Medicare. those
politically protected categories are
growing almost double digits on a
compound growth rate basis whereas you
know which is you know that stuff's
about 70% of the budget deficit. So the
other 30% of the budget deficit has to
compound down roughly 20% in order to
keep the budget deficit flat. And so
this has nothing to do with Doge or
anything to do with the tax reform or
the tax the tax bill. It's just the math
of of a aging economy and the math of
recycling your your debt to a higher
interest rate regime. Those are just two
factors that are ongoing uh and are
likely to continue to apply upper
pressure on the budget deficit over a
medium-term time over the long-term time
horizon. So uh as much as they want to
talk tough about fiscal retrenchment,
the reality is is paradigm C won't exist
if they are serious about fiscal
retrenchment in a way that you know over
the let's say over the next three to
four years takes the budget deficit from
let's call it 7% of GDP towards you know
bestins 3% of GDP. We think that's a
pipe dream and it's very unlikely to
happen. um incredibly unlikely to happen
just based on everything we just said.
Um but the reality is we could go from
7% down to six or five and back up to
seven or even back up to eight uh just
over the course of this this this Trump
administration's um tenure as a function
for all those dynamics that we're
highlighting. But at the end of the day,
it's bullish. You're talking about
pumping more and more trillions of
dollars into the economy from the growth
of the budget deficit. Uh that's been
the case uh for years now. And that's
what part of the reason we continue to
see um that's what part of the reason
the US has been exceptional and is
likely to remain exceptional uh from an
economic standpoint uh to the extent
that paradigm C is the highest
probability outcome and as we said we do
believe paradigm C is the highest
probability outcome. So uh finally I
think that that was it. So yeah I'll
wrap it up there. I think I went on
pretty long enough for this but uh uh
appreciate the always super thoughtful
questions that are in our community. Uh
even more so than the questions, I
appreciate that we have thousands of
investors, you know, asking and
answering each other's questions in here
and we're all learning together and
trying to get to the right answer uh
faster than uh um communities outside of
our pay wall. We're really appreciative
for that. Appreciative of, you know,
each one of each one of you guys here on
team 42. Uh and just want to say thanks
for tuning in to these. We'll catch you
back here uh tomorrow. Cheers.
